title: Lentil and bacon soup
subtitle: Green lentils and smoked bacon in a thyme-scented broth.
excerpt: Smoked streaky bacon rendered down with onion and carrot, then simmered with green lentils and chicken stock until the lentils are soft and the broth is deeply savoury. A thick, filling soup that needs nothing alongside but bread.
sourceNotes: Lentil and bacon soups have been a feature of British home cooking for centuries, drawing on the long tradition of using cured pork to flavour pulse-based soups. Split pea and ham soup and lentil soup with bacon appear in nineteenth-century British cookery books as economical winter soups. The modern version with green or brown lentils and smoked streaky bacon is the standard British home-cook form.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions for up to three months. Reheat gently with a splash of water.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in the fridge for four days. Flavour improves on the second day.

content.text: Smoked bacon is the backbone of this soup. Rendered slowly in a dry pan before anything else goes in, it gives the soup a depth of flavour that stock alone cannot match. The fat that renders out becomes the cooking medium for the onion and carrot.

Green or brown lentils hold their shape better than red lentils and give the soup more texture. They do not need soaking and cook through in 30–35 minutes. The soup does not need blending — it is a chunky, satisfying broth.
A large, heavy-based saucepan is the only kit required. No blender needed.

Place the bacon lardons in a cold large saucepan. Set over a medium heat and cook for 5–6 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the fat has rendered and the lardons are beginning to crisp. No oil is needed.

Add the onion, carrot, and celery to the bacon fat in the pan. Cook for 8 minutes over a medium heat, stirring occasionally, until softened. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and cook for 2 minutes more.

Add {{lentils-green}} of rinsed green lentils, {{thyme}} of thyme, and {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of hot chicken stock. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a steady simmer. Cook for 30–35 minutes until the lentils are completely tender. Remove the thyme stalks and bay leaves. Season with {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Taste before adding salt: the bacon and stock may have provided enough. Serve with chopped parsley.

items.cause: Old lentils, or stock that was too acidic or too salty from the start.
items.fix: Add more hot water and continue simmering. Salt added too early in cooking can prevent lentils from softening; add it only towards the end.

items.cause: Smoked bacon and stock cubes both contribute significant salt.
items.fix: Add more water and more lentils. Use low-salt stock next time.
Spoon off any excess fat before serving. Some fat is expected and carries flavour, but very fatty streaky bacon can leave too much.

items.cause: Lentils not cooked long enough to break down partially.
items.fix: Cook for another 10 minutes and stir to encourage some lentils to break down naturally.

content.text: Lentil and bacon soup is one of the enduring British working-lunch soups: filling, inexpensive to make in quantity, and better the day after. The combination of cured pork and dried pulses appears in virtually every European cooking tradition, and the British version draws on both the French soupe aux lentilles and the German Linsensuppe without being either. It is midwinter food: plain and reliable.
